title: What to know about Chelidonium majus (celandine) before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Chelidonium majus helps healing several common ailments, such as digestive issues, respiratory problems, and skin irritations. The plant's active compounds, including alkaloids and glycosides, have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that make it a natural remedy for various health complaints. Specifically, Chelidonium majus has been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of indigestion, bloating, and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) by soothing the digestive tract and reducing inflammation. It also shows promise in treating respiratory conditions such as bronchitis, asthma, and chronic coughs due to its ability to relax airway muscles and reduce mucus production. Additionally, Chelidonium majus has been used topically to treat skin issues like eczema, acne, and dermatitis, where it helps to reduce redness, itching, and inflammation, promoting a healthy and balanced skin microbiome. Overall, the versatile benefits of Chelidonium majus make it a popular natural remedy for addressing various common health concerns.
section_medicine_properties: Chelidonium majus has several medicinal properties, such as anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antiviral activities, which make it a valuable herb in traditional medicine. The most important property of this plant is its ability to stimulate the liver and digestive system, making it effective in treating a range of conditions including jaundice, hepatitis, and gastrointestinal disorders. Chelidonium majus contains alkaloids such as berberine, which has been shown to have potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, reducing inflammation and pain associated with conditions like arthritis and gout. Additionally, the herb's antibacterial and antiviral properties make it effective against a range of infections, including respiratory tract infections, urinary tract infections, and herpes simplex virus. Furthermore, Chelidonium majus has also been traditionally used to treat skin conditions such as eczema, acne, and dermatitis due to its ability to reduce inflammation and improve wound healing. Overall, the medicinal properties of Chelidonium majus make it a valuable herb in the treatment of various health disorders, and its use is often recommended by herbalists and naturopaths.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Chelidonium majus for medicinal purposes are the leaves, stems, and roots. The leaves contain a high concentration of alkaloids, particularly berberine, which is responsible for the plant's anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antiviral properties. Berberine has been shown to be effective in treating a range of conditions, including digestive issues such as dyspepsia and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), as well as respiratory problems like bronchitis and pneumonia. The stems and roots of the plant also contain berberine, although in smaller quantities than the leaves. These parts are often used to make decoctions or tinctures that can be taken orally to treat a variety of ailments, including urinary tract infections, kidney stones, and liver and gallbladder issues. Additionally, Chelidonium majus has been traditionally used topically to treat skin conditions such as eczema, acne, and wounds, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Chelidonium majus increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as gastrointestinal disturbances, including nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain. This herb can also cause allergic reactions in some individuals, manifesting as skin rash, itching, or even anaphylaxis in severe cases. Furthermore, high doses or prolonged use of Chelidonium majus may lead to liver damage, gallbladder issues, and even kidney problems due to its ability to interact with certain medications and disrupt normal bodily functions. Additionally, some users may experience dizziness, headache, or fatigue when using this herb without proper guidance or medical supervision. In rare cases, improper use of Chelidonium majus has been linked to more serious complications, such as increased risk of bleeding due to platelet dysfunction, which can be particularly problematic for individuals with pre-existing bleeding disorders. It is essential to consult with a qualified healthcare professional before using Chelidonium majus or any other herbal remedy to minimize the risk of adverse effects and ensure safe and effective treatment.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Chelidonium majus medicinally are crucial for ensuring safe and effective use of this herb. First and foremost, individuals with liver or kidney disease should avoid using Chelidonium majus, as it may exacerbate these conditions. Additionally, people taking medications that affect the liver, such as antidepressants or blood thinners, should consult with a healthcare professional before using the herb, as it may interact with these medications. It is also essential to monitor liver function tests regularly while using Chelidonium majus, especially at high doses or for extended periods of time. Furthermore, women who are pregnant or breastfeeding should use caution when consuming this herb, as its effects on fetal and infant development are not well understood. Finally, individuals with gallstones or bile duct obstruction should avoid using Chelidonium majus, as it may stimulate the release of bile, potentially exacerbating these conditions. By taking these precautions, individuals can minimize the risk of adverse reactions and maximize the benefits of using Chelidonium majus medicinally.
section_horticulture: Chelidonium majus, also known as Celandine poppy, is a perennial herb that thrives in well-draining, moist soils and partial to full shade conditions. It requires a slightly acidic to neutral soil pH, ranging from 6.0 to 7.0, which allows it to absorb essential nutrients efficiently. The ideal temperature for growth lies between 15°C and 25°C (59°F-77°F), making it a suitable choice for temperate climates with moderate summers. Chelidonium majus can tolerate some drought, but consistent moisture levels promote healthy leaf growth and flower production. In terms of light requirements, the plant can grow well in areas receiving partial shade to full sun, although excessive direct sunlight may cause scorching or wilting. By providing these optimal horticultural conditions, gardeners can encourage Chelidonium majus to flourish, producing its characteristic yellow flowers and attractive, maple-like foliage.
section_horticulture_growth: Chelidonium majus, also known as greater celandine or wild heliotrope, is a perennial plant that requires specific growth conditions to thrive. It typically grows best in well-drained, rich soil with a slightly acidic to neutral pH (5.5-7.0). The plant prefers full sun to partial shade and can tolerate a range of moisture levels, although it performs better when the soil is consistently moist but not waterlogged. Chelidonium majus grows up to 1-2 meters in height and spreads by rhizomes, making it ideal for naturalized areas or cottage gardens. It requires minimal maintenance and can thrive in urban environments, provided the soil is not too compacted and the plant receives adequate light. In terms of pruning, Chelidonium majus benefits from regular trimming to promote bushy growth and prevent flowering, which can occur as early as spring if the plant is not pruned regularly. Overall, with proper growing conditions and minimal care, Chelidonium majus can be a low-maintenance yet striking addition to any garden or landscape.
section_horticulture_planting: Chelidonium majus, also known as greater celandine, is a perennial plant that requires specific planting conditions to thrive. When planting Celandine, it's essential to choose a location with full sun to partial shade and well-draining soil that is rich in organic matter. The ideal temperature for germination is between 65°F to 75°F (18°C to 24°C), so fall or early spring planting is recommended. Sow the seeds directly into the ground, about 1/4 inch deep and 1-2 inches apart, after the last frost date in your area. Water gently but thoroughly after planting and keep the soil consistently moist during the first growing season. As the plants mature, you can reduce watering to once a week, allowing the top inch of soil to dry out between waterings. Avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot and other problems. Mulching around the base of the plants can help retain moisture and suppress weeds, while also providing a decorative touch to your garden.
section_horticulture_caring: Chelidonium majus, also known as Celandine poppy or Greater celandine, is a delicate and sensitive herb that requires specific care to thrive. To promote healthy growth and prevent potential issues, it's essential to provide Chelidonium majus with well-draining soil and full sun to partial shade conditions. The plant prefers slightly acidic to neutral soil pH and should be planted in a spot with good air circulation to prevent fungal diseases. Watering should be done carefully, as the herb is susceptible to root rot if the soil becomes too waterlogged. Fertilization can be done during the growing season, but it's crucial to use a balanced fertilizer at half the recommended strength to avoid burning the roots. Pruning is also essential, as Chelidonium majus has a tendency to sprawl and produce fewer flowers if not kept under control. Regular removal of spent flowers and deadhead leaves will encourage bushy growth and promote more blooms. By following these caring tips, you can enjoy the beautiful yellow flowers and medicinal benefits of this versatile herb.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Chelidonium majus, also known as Celandine, is a perennial herb that requires careful harvesting to ensure maximum potency and quality. The ideal time for harvesting is in late spring or early summer when the plant is in full flower, as this is when the highest concentration of alkaloids is present. To harvest the leaves, stems, and flowers, gently grasp the stem about an inch from the ground and twist it counterclockwise to avoid damaging the roots. Cut off the entire stem just above a node, as cutting too much of the plant can weaken it and reduce its ability to regrow. It is essential to dry or process the harvested material quickly to prevent the loss of volatile oils, which are responsible for many of Celandine's medicinal properties. Additionally, it is recommended to harvest from plants grown in rich, fertile soil, as this will result in a more robust and resilient plant with higher levels of active compounds.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Chelidonium majus, also known as greater celandine, is a plant species that is native to Europe and Asia. While it has been used in traditional medicine for centuries, it can be susceptible to various pests and diseases that can impact its growth and productivity. One of the most common pests affecting Chelidonium majus is the aphid, which feeds on the sap of the leaves and stems, causing curled or distorted foliage. Additionally, the plant is also prone to infestations by slugs and snails, which can chew holes in the leaves and stems, leading to significant damage. From a disease perspective, Chelidonium majus is susceptible to fungal diseases such as leaf spot and powdery mildew, which can cause yellowing or browning of the leaves and stems. It can also be affected by root rot caused by excessive moisture and poor drainage, which can lead to stunted growth and reduced yields. Furthermore, Chelidonium majus is also susceptible to nematodes, microscopic worms that feed on the plant's roots, causing reduced water uptake and stunted growth. Effective management of these pests and diseases requires a combination of good cultural practices, such as maintaining proper soil drainage and providing adequate air circulation, along with integrated pest management strategies that incorporate biological control agents, chemical pesticides, and resistant cultivars.
section_botany: Chelidonium majus, also known as greater celandine or chelidonia, is an herbaceous perennial plant that belongs to the poppy family (Papaveraceae). Botanically, it features a distinctive appearance with bright yellow flowers and four-petaled petals that are 2-3 cm in diameter. The stems of the plant are hairy and quadrangular, growing up to 30-60 cm tall, with deeply lobed leaves that have an oblong or ovate shape and measure 10-20 cm long. The leaves have a distinctive glossy surface and a pointed tip, with three to seven lobes on either side of the midrib. The plant produces yellow flowers in late spring and early summer, which are followed by spherical seed pods containing several seeds. The roots of Chelidonium majus are also notable for their distinctive property of staining orange when cut or bruised due to the presence of a bright orange-colored latex. This unique characteristic has been used as an identifier in traditional herbalism.
section_botany_taxonomy: Chelidonium majus belongs to the kingdom Plantae, division Magnoliophyta, class Magnoliopsida, order Ranunculales, family Papaveraceae, and genus Chelidonium. Specifically, it is a perennial herb species known as Greater Celandine or Wild Celery, native to Europe and Western Asia.
section_botany_variants: Chelidonium majus has variants, such as C. m. subsp. majus (typical or common celandine) and C. m. subsp. panormitanum (Sicilian celandine). Additionally, there are several forms and cultivars, including 'Aureum', 'Album', and 'Rubrum', which exhibit variations in leaf shape, color, and flower hue. Some sources also recognize a fourth subspecies, C. m. subsp. montanum, found in the mountains of southern Europe. These variants can be distinguished based on morphological characteristics such as leaf shape, flower size, and habit.
section_botany_distribution: Chelidonium majus, also known as Greater Celandine or Poppy-of-the-North, is a perennial plant native to Europe, Western Asia, and parts of Northern Africa. Its natural range extends from the Mediterranean region in the south to Norway and Russia in the north, and from Great Britain and Ireland in the west to Central Asia and China in the east. It typically thrives in temperate regions with moist soils, often growing in forests, woodland edges, and meadows at elevations up to 1,500 meters above sea level.
section_botany_life_cycle: Chelidonium majus, also known as Greater Celandine, is an annual or biennial herb that completes its life cycle within a year or two. The plant starts by germinating from seed in early spring, producing small yellow flowers in May and June. After pollination, the flowers develop into seed pods that split open to release numerous seeds, often scattering them far away from the parent plant. As summer fades, the plant's energy is directed towards producing seeds, causing the leaves and stems to wither and die. The next year, new seeds germinate, starting the cycle again.
section_history: Chelidonium majus, also known as greater celandine, has a rich history dating back to ancient Greece and Rome. It was used as a medicinal plant to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and snakebites. The plant's leaves were used to make an ointment for wounds, while its seeds were believed to have antiseptic properties. In traditional European folk medicine, Chelidonium majus was also used to treat digestive issues and as a natural remedy for skin conditions.
section_history_mythology: Chelidonium majus, also known as greater celandine, has been steeped in mythological significance across various cultures. In ancient Greek mythology, it was associated with Asclepius, the god of healing, as its sap was believed to have healing properties. Similarly, in European folklore, Chelidonium was thought to possess magical powers, with some believing it could ward off evil spirits and others using it to treat a range of ailments.
section_history_simbology: Chelidonium majus, also known as Greater Celandine, has been imbued with various symbolic meanings across cultures. In alchemy and magic, it's associated with transformation, renewal, and spiritual growth, often depicted in illustrations of the Great Work. In herbalism, its yellow sap is believed to possess purifying properties, symbolizing cleansing and protection from negative energies. Additionally, the plant's bright colors and star-shaped flowers are thought to represent hope, optimism, and divine guidance.
section_history_literature: Chelidonium majus, also known as greater celandine, has been used in traditional medicine for centuries. Historical texts such as Dioscorides' "De Materia Medica" (1st century AD) and Pliny the Elder's "Naturalis Historia" (1st century AD) mention its medicinal properties. The 16th-century herbalist Matthiolus wrote extensively about its uses in his book "Commentarii in Pedacii Dioscoridis Anazarbei de Materia Medica". These texts highlight the plant's reputation for treating various ailments, including skin conditions and eye problems.
section_history_artifacts: Chelidonium majus, also known as Greater Celandine, has a rich history dating back thousands of years. Archaeologists have discovered ancient artifacts such as clay figurines and pottery shards depicting the plant's distinctive yellow flowers and leaves. Additionally, historical records from the Middle Ages reveal that Chelidonium was used in traditional medicine and magic spells, earning it the nickname "witch's weed."
intro_desc: Chelidonium majus, commonly known as celandine, is a herb that boasts a multitude of health properties, from anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefits to improved digestion and reduced stress levels, making it a valuable addition to any wellness routine. Cultivated for its ornamental and medicinal value, the Chelidonium majus plant is relatively easy to grow, thriving in a variety of conditions and requiring minimal maintenance. From a botanical standpoint, this herb belongs to the Papaveraceae family and is characterized by its bright yellow flowers and large, ovate leaves, which contain a unique alkaloid profile. The ancient Greeks and Romans prized Chelidonium majus for its medicinal properties, and it has since been used throughout history in various forms, including as a tea, tincture, and topical application.
medicine_desc: Chelidonium majus helps with treating various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions such as eczema and acne. Its medicinal properties also extend to treating gastrointestinal issues like indigestion and constipation.

The plant's active constituents, including berberine and protoberberine alkaloids, are responsible for its medicinal properties. These compounds exhibit anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antiviral activities. Berberine, in particular, is a key component, displaying potent antimicrobial effects.

The root and rhizome of Chelidonium majus are most commonly used for medicinal purposes. The root is rich in alkaloids, including berberine and palmatine, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. The rhizome, on the other hand, is often used to treat skin conditions and promote wound healing.

Improper use of Chelidonium majus can lead to side effects, including gastrointestinal disturbances, allergic reactions, and photosensitivity. Prolonged use may also lead to interactions with other medications and exacerbate underlying conditions.

Precautions when using Chelidonium majus medicinally include monitoring blood pressure and sugar levels, as well as being aware of potential interactions with other medications. Pregnant and breastfeeding women should consult a healthcare professional before using the plant, and individuals with allergies or sensitivities should exercise caution.
horticulture_desc: Chelidonium majus grow best in partial shade and moist, fertile soils with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0. It thrives in woodland environments and can tolerate a range of soil types, from acidic to alkaline. The plant requires adequate water but is drought-tolerant once established.

For optimal growth, plant Chelidonium majus in spring or fall, when temperatures range from 10°C to 20°C (50°F to 68°F). Sow seeds 1-2 cm deep and 30 cm apart in a nursery bed or directly in the garden. Transplant seedlings 15-20 cm deep and 30-45 cm apart when they are 20-25 cm tall.

To harvest Chelidonium majus, collect the leaves and flowers as needed, or wait for the whole plant to die back in autumn. Dig up the roots in late winter or early spring, when they are dormant, and dry them for later use. Harvesting the plant regularly encourages new growth.

Common pests affecting Chelidonium majus include aphids, caterpillars, and slugs. Root rot and leaf spot diseases, caused by fungi such as Fusarium and Septoria, can also occur. Regularly inspect plants for signs of infestation or infection and treat promptly to prevent the spread of disease.
botany_desc: Chelidonium majus is a perennial herbaceous plant that belongs to the Papaveraceae family. Its stem is erect, glabrous, and quadrangular, reaching heights of 30-90 cm. Leaves are alternate, ovate, and cordate, with 3-5 pairs of lobes and a petiole that is 1-3 cm long.

Chelidonium majus is classified as follows: Kingdom: Plantae, Clade: Angiosperms, Clade: Eudicots, Clade: Asterids, Order: Ranunculales, Family: Papaveraceae, Genus: Chelidonium, Species: C. majus. The plant is sometimes placed in the Berberidopsidales order.

Variants of Chelidonium majus include: Chelidonium majus var. majus, Chelidonium majus var. corymbiferum, and Chelidonium majus var. montanum. These variants differ in their leaf shape, stem length, and flowering habits. Some sources also recognize Chelidonium hybridum as a distinct variant.

Chelidonium majus is native to Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa. It has been naturalized in many parts of the world, including North America, where it has become an invasive species in some areas. The plant grows in a variety of habitats, including woodlands, meadows, and along streams.

The life cycle of Chelidonium majus begins with germination in the spring, when seeds are triggered to grow by warming temperatures. The plant reaches maturity in mid-summer, producing yellow flowers that are rich in alkaloids and other bioactive compounds. Fruits are formed in late summer, containing seeds that can remain viable for many years.
history_desc: Chelidonium majus is a perennial herb that has been used medicinally for centuries. Its roots and leaves were used to treat various ailments, including skin conditions, fever, and digestive issues. In medieval Europe, it was used to treat the "English disease," a term used to describe a condition characterized by a painful swelling in the groin.

In ancient Greek and Roman mythology, celandine was associated with the sun god, Apollo, and was said to have the power to ward off evil spirits. The plant was also linked to the goddess of love, Aphrodite, and was used in rituals to promote fertility and passion.

In medieval herbology, celandine was associated with the element of fire and was used to symbolize purification, protection, and healing. It was also used in spells to ward off evil spirits and to promote spiritual growth. In some traditions, celandine is still used as a protective charm against witchcraft and evil eye.

In the 16th century, German physician Leonhart Fuchs described celandine in his book "De Historia Stirpium," noting its medicinal properties and uses. In the 17th century, English herbalist John Gerard included celandine in his book "Herball," describing its uses for treating various ailments.

Historical artifacts depicting celandine include a 16th-century woodcut by German artist and botanist Leonhart Fuchs, which shows the plant's distinctive yellow flowers and heart-shaped leaves. A 17th-century painting by Dutch artist Jan Brueghel the Younger features celandine as a prominent element in a still life composition.
